---
title: "Peclet Examples"
subtitle: "See what's possible — then copy, run, and adapt."
toc: false
---

Worked, **runnable** examples for the [`peclet`](https://pypi.org/project/peclet/)
simulation suite: GPU-accelerated particle dynamics, CFD, and the spatial-indexing
primitives they build on. Every example is **self-contained** (it builds its own
geometry and sets every parameter inline), executed to produce the figures and
numbers you see, and one click from running in the browser.

## Gallery

::: {.grid}

::: {.g-col-12 .g-col-md-6}
### [Plane Poiseuille — cut-cell IBM is exact →](examples/poiseuille-ibm/index.qmd)
Body-force channel flow past immersed no-slip walls, reproduced to machine
precision — and the validation trap that makes it *look* inexact.

*flow · cut-cell IBM · SDF · verification*
:::

::: {.g-col-12 .g-col-md-6}
### [Developing channel flow →](examples/developing-channel/index.qmd)
A uniform stream enters a channel and relaxes into the parabolic Poiseuille
profile — the classic entrance region, using inflow/outflow boundaries.

*flow · inflow/outflow · boundary conditions*
:::

::: {.g-col-12 .g-col-md-6}
### [Backward-facing step →](examples/backward-facing-step/index.qmd)
A stream expands over a step, separates, and reattaches downstream: the canonical
separated-flow benchmark, with the step realized purely as an inlet profile and the
reattachment length tracked vs Reynolds number.

*flow · inflow/outflow · separation · benchmark*
:::

::: {.g-col-12 .g-col-md-6}
### [Pipe flow — second-order convergence →](examples/pipe-poiseuille/index.qmd)
The same parabolic flow through a *curved* cylindrical wall, where the cut-cell
IBM finally shows its genuine O(h²) accuracy — the convergence study the plane
channel can't give.

*flow · cut-cell IBM · SDF · convergence*
:::

::: {.g-col-12 .g-col-md-6}
### [Taylor–Green vortex →](examples/taylor-green/index.qmd)
An exact unsteady Navier–Stokes solution: advection injects divergence, the
projection removes it (to ~1e-15), and the kinetic energy decays at exactly the
analytic viscous rate.

*flow · projection · unsteady · verification*
:::

::: {.g-col-12 .g-col-md-6}
### [Lid-driven cavity vs Ghia →](examples/lid-driven-cavity/index.qmd)
The most-cited wall-bounded benchmark: a moving lid, a primary vortex and corner
eddies, with centreline profiles matched to the classic Ghia et al. (1982) data.

*flow · boundary conditions · benchmark*
:::

::: {.g-col-12 .g-col-md-6}
### [Zick–Homsy sphere arrays →](examples/zick-homsy/index.qmd)
Creeping flow through periodic sphere packings: the cut-cell drag grid-converges
to the classic Zick & Homsy (1982) benchmark, across solid fractions and cubic
lattices (SC/BCC/FCC).

*flow · Stokes · porous media · benchmark*
:::

::: {.g-col-12 .g-col-md-6}
### [Cylinder vortex street →](examples/cylinder-vortex-street/index.qmd)
Unsteady flow past a confined cylinder on the Schäfer–Turek benchmark geometry:
a periodic Kármán vortex street, with the Strouhal number and pressure drop.

*flow · inflow/outflow · IBM · unsteady · benchmark*
:::

::: {.g-col-12 .g-col-md-6}
### [Random packed bed (dem → flow) →](examples/random-packed-bed/index.qmd)
The first cross-method example: build a random close packing with `peclet.dem`,
characterise it (porosity, coordination, g(r)), then push Stokes flow through the
pores with `peclet.flow` and measure permeability vs Carman–Kozeny.

*dem · flow · porous media · multi-method*
:::

::: {.g-col-12 .g-col-md-6}
### [A ring packed-bed permeability study →](examples/ring-packed-bed/index.qmd)
What a small scientific study looks like end-to-end: pack hollow-cylinder (Raschig)
rings with `peclet.dem`, run cut-cell Stokes flow with `peclet.flow`, validate
(grid convergence, resolution guardrail, realization statistics), sweep the shape,
and collapse every ring onto one Kozeny–Carman law.

*dem · flow · porous media · permeability · study · DOE*
:::

::: {.g-col-12 .g-col-md-6}
### [Custom-shaped particles (dem, SDF) →](examples/sdf-particle-packing/index.qmd)
Beyond spheres: author a particle as a signed distance function (a dumbbell), turn
it into a DEM particle — surface shell, moment of inertia and all — with
`build_particle`, and pack a boxful.

*dem · particles · SDF · non-spherical · packing*
:::

::: {.g-col-12 .g-col-md-6}
### [Rotating drum (dem, moving SDF walls) →](examples/rotating-drum/index.qmd)
The *container* is an SDF too: confine grains in a drum written as a signed distance
function, give the wall a velocity it never actually moves at, and watch friction
carry the bed up the rising side to a dynamic angle of repose.

*dem · SDF · walls · granular · rotating-drum · friction*
:::

:::

*(More examples are landing here through the night: cylindrical pipe flow with
genuine second-order convergence, Zick–Homsy Stokes drag through sphere arrays,
flow past a cylinder and its vortex street, the lid-driven cavity vs Ghia, the
Taylor–Green vortex, packed beds built with `peclet.dem`, and more.)*

## Characterization & benchmarks

Beyond *what* the suite can do, a companion set of pages measures *how well* it does
it — accuracy (order of convergence and discretization bias), performance, and
parallel scaling. The first: a head-to-head **[staggered vs collocated grid
convergence](benchmarks/staggered-vs-collocated/index.qmd)** of the Stokes drag
through a sphere array — same problem, two velocity placements, and the ~1% accuracy
gap that survives mesh refinement. See the **[benchmarks overview](benchmarks/index.qmd)**.

::: {.callout-note}
## How these run
Each example installs `peclet` from PyPI in its first cell, so it runs on a free
Colab CPU runtime out of the box. Solver-backed outputs are also **frozen** into
this site, so the pages always build without a GPU.
:::
